speaker: interviewer
text: 20th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia. And the camera's ready.

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank, you were telling me that you'd got better in the hospital. What happened after that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I got better. It was probably around the 1st of September, approximately, could be couple days before or after. Anyway, one morning, four tall SS officers came in, impeccably groomed, need I say, with their [? haute ?] [? cologne ?] and boots. And they said, [SPEAKING GERMAN], everybody naked, out from the beds. Stand to attention on the wall. So everybody had to stand to attention. And then they went around. And we need particularly healthy young men for medical experimentation. At that thought, I-- I nearly fainted, because I already knew what happened with the previous lot. They nearly all died or legs cut off, arms cut off. They got malaria and yellow fever and all sorts of diseases injected into them. But they-- we were about 400 there-- so we need 20, 24, or 28, I'm not sure. Anyhow, he went around picking out young men, mostly young people between 20 and 30. And he already had quite a few and say, oh, need one more. [SPEAKING GERMAN] And then he came up to me and said, ah, you, you're [INAUDIBLE]. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he went up. He went up to the block commander and said-- he took all the numbers-- said, you are going to be transferred from here to another block where there will be medical experimentation carried out from you. You'll go over there tomorrow morning. So then they went away. So I said, that's terrible, bad luck. But actually, fact is, it was the-- the biggest good luck which happened to me, as it will turn out later in a couple of minutes. I thought it's terrible bad luck. I was sure I'm going to die in a very painful way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I just had enough time to go to some of my friends and told them that, if by some miracle you survive it, would you tell my parents when you get home or, you know, my family, if you get home, that I-- I perished here in September '44 with medical-- probably I get malaria or some other horrible diseases injected into my body and so forth. I was sure I'm going to die.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, next day we went over to the other block-- we went-- we went-- that was opposite the other one. It was-- I think it was number 28 we went to. It was the end of Auschwitz. From there was just a wide fence to be seen, all that was on the corner of the Auschwitz camp past-- we could only see out through the wires and then the outside perimeter of the camp. It was hidden away from the other buildings.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there he put us in this room, all the 24 of us. And we got better food. He ordered special, good food to be brought in because he wanted the experiments to be carried out under, you know, normal, healthy-- on healthy people, what-- what diseases he can-- what the [INAUDIBLE] can cause to healthy people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we had double amount of bread, and the-- the soup actually had potato pieces in it, which was fantastic. So we-- we didn't suffer so much from it, and we didn't have to do anything, just lie on the beds.

speaker: interviewee
text: And every day, he would call out one or two. His name was Dr. [? Schumacher, ?] SS captain. And he had a Jewish helper, a doctor who had to assist him and do what operations he had to [INAUDIBLE] on. But he was, of course, doing it together with him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So every day, he took  down two-- two boys, three boys, two in the morning, two in the afternoon. And they came back with terrible pains. They-- they-- they put some pus into their arms and in the-- in their legs. We don't know what-- what he put in, but it must have been terribly painful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And other boys had to swallow four yellow tablets every hour, and they developed malaria, huge, high temperatures, and all sorts of diseases. I mean, we-- he didn't give us the details what he did, but there were-- there were people all crying all night and, ooh, you know, in terrible pains. This went on like that for weeks and weeks. There was a-- a Polish doctor, a Polish Christian doctor, who was there. He was trying to help us and give us what he could. Sometimes he could get some aspirin for the pain, not always, but he-- he did his best. He was-- he was very helpful. And I had long discussions with him, because that was the time when the Warsaw uprising, not the Jewish uprising. That was the previous year. This was the-- the Christian uprising of Warsaw. And then we even-- we discussed military position in Warsaw, how the fights is going on. And later on this friendship was very important for me, because he saved my life later on. But anyhow, eventually the day came when they called me out, and that was the last experiment of this series. And to my colossal good luck, all he did is I had to lie on my stomach, and I got a slight ether-- so a bit-- a bits put to sleep almost. I mean, I didn't feel it so strongly but still very painful. And they cut out a big piece of flesh from my left leg, which can be still seen. I don't know. And this was about approximately 18 centimeters by 12 centimeters. He cut it out real deep. And then when the cut was made, then he sprinkled a generous around of kerosene or petrol. I'm not sure which one, one or the other. It was extremely-- I-- I fainted from the pain. And then he put a bandage on, and they left the kerosene settle into the blood. And all night, I couldn't sleep. It was terrible painful. I was crying. It was, you know, moaning, like all the others were also moaning, so it was nothing special. It was horrible days. And then every second day, he came back to everybody, checked the conditions, took temperatures. In my case, I had to go up to him on one leg-- I couldn't stand on that leg, of course-- and then he had a look at it. He took-- he took out his Leica camera. He took snapshots. He had already a-- a whole dossier with my photos on, how the pus develops, how this gets more and more swollen every day. Then he put another dose of kerosene back on it. So then he took another photo the next day and the day after. And it went on like that. It was terrible. But after about three weeks-- could be three or it could be four weeks-- end of September, by that time, he finished. He packed up all his files, his photos, and he finished. No more kerosene. And then the Jewish doctor, who was-- who remained there-- he-- he remained. The SS, he went away, but the Jewish doctor-- then he took off the thing, and then he put some cream on it, some healing creams. And then it's-- the slow healing process started. He put a heavy bandage on it. I couldn't walk yet. But with great difficulty, I could hop out to the toilet if I had to. And the big event was, of course, that the rumor went around by the last week of September that there will be a big selection on the 1st of October, a big selection, which every three months happens in this camp, 20,000 people. And they will pull out the weakest 2,000 or 3,000, send them over to the gas chamber, and they-- they replenish the stock with fresh new arrivals, healthy, strong men.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they had to find out which are the-- those people who are no-- not really much useful for work-- for work in the coal mines or carrying bags of cement or so forth. Well, I certainly wasn't fit to carry bags of cement. I couldn't hardly go out to the toilet.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they came. We-- we heard the news, because the first day they checked all the regular blocks. People had to run around 600 meters, and the-- the ones who couldn't run fast enough at-- at making a-- a full oval, then they were popped out, and then they were put on the list-- gas chamber-- and gas, you know. And the same night-- they didn't come to the hospital-- they were the-- the same night, one of my best friends-- a 36-year-old man, a factory owner in Budapest-- he came over to me to the hospital block-- to the-- to my block, and he said, imagine, they took-- put me on the list. Tomorrow morning, I'm going to the gas chamber at 7 o'clock. He was perspiring. And oh, I will take a deep breath-- and so I-- and should you get back, any of you, because there's several boys there, tell my wife that that's how I died. And that's how he died. He was sweating. Imagine telling somebody, tomorrow morning you go to a gas chamber. And he went. He did-- he went to the gas chamber. They picked out hundreds and hundreds and hundreds and, I mean, from every block, and then they had the required number. And then they came to the hospital block. Now, there they really had a field day, because hardly anyone was well enough to do any work for-- for Hitler and the Third Reich. So first, they went to the downstairs room. They nearly put everybody on the list. And then they stopped and they say, tomorrow morning we'll do the upstairs rooms. And I was in the upstairs room. Well, in that night, I was up all night. I was just trying to exercise my bad leg, see if I could walk a bit on it. And with some effort, I-- I learned to walk, because it was still terribly sore. But I-- I managed to walk up and down a bit. And I said, well, no way. They can't select me for work. It's impossible. But you know, I tried to do my best and, you know, whatever I could do.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I thought-- thought about it. Well, if they select me, I'll just go to the gas chamber. I-- what will it be? Just take three deep breaths and-- and-- and then pass out. So that's it. What's-- what's the big deal? It's-- it won't be as bad as-- as going to the dentist. Just take three deep breath, and you don't know anything more. So I was ready for it. The next day, they came, two of them-- ah, [SPEAKING GERMAN], one by one. And then say, oh, he can't move. He has high temperature, malaria. Put him on the list. And the next one, put him on the list. Put him on the list. You know, he-- he selected-- 9 out of 10 went on the gas chamber list. And then he came to my bed. And well, he said, [SPEAKING GERMAN]? And-- and they understand me. And what-- what's-- what's up with you? Can you go to work? Come on, jump out from bed. Up-- come up here. Could you walk? Said, oh, sure, sure I can walk. When are you going back to work? I said, ah, three or four days, I'll be back at work for sure. Say, all right. Well, just walk up there and back. And I walked up and back as-- [INAUDIBLE] [LAUGHS], you know, as fast as I could. Terrific pain, but, you know, I didn't show it. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: OK, OK. Well, you make sure you are back at work in three days time. And he didn't put me on the list. They put nearly all of the others. Maybe-- maybe four didn't get on the list there. 20-- 20 went on the list in  our room. And the next morning-- together with my friend who already lost his leg in the other room-- in the morning the trucks came. The ones who couldn't walk up on the truck, they just-- two SS men holding at one end and the other end-- threw them up like bags of potato. They just flew on up from the floor to the truck, tossed in, bang down. High-temperature cases and lost legs and arms or whatever, who couldn't walk up on the truck, was thrown up and thrown in. And after about two hours, the trucks came back with the shoes and the uniforms to be washed. And they were already gassed, finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I was alive. I think some factor could have been that I had very blonde hair in those days. Now, I'm darker, but I was very light blonde with blue eyes and very white skin. So the SS man could have thought that maybe I'm just a-- a German criminal. I mean, he didn't analyze me-- who am I? I could-- there were Germans amongst us. German-- there were Germans who were homosexual, Germans who were priests, Germans who were socialists or communists. There were Germans who were just-- who had just murdered their father or-- or hold up a bank or criminals, you know? So he could have thought, if for one second, that, oh, he's OK. I mean, he's blonde and, you know. That was-- if anybody who had black hair or dark skin-- that-- that was a Jew, for sure. But anybody blonde, that could-- could have been a Christian. So that's how I survived it. And it was a miracle. It was absolute miracle.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the second miracle came couple of days after, when the commandant of the-- of the building, a-- a German Christian-- he was a former socialist leader or something. He was about 55 year old. He was a real socialist. And he said, look, Frank, I won't let you go. I was thinking about it. You are due to go out to work next week when your leg will get a bit better. With the bandage on it's all right. They want you to go back to work, but I won't let you go back to work. I'll see to it that you get a job right here in this building-- in this building. And the next morning he says, your job will be with a different uniform. You get a-- a thing on your-- insignia. You are going to be Leichentrager and in charge of the [GERMAN], which is the morgue-- the-- the morgue. And if anybody is beaten to death or collapses, dies, you and three others, you have to run there with your stretcher, pick up the dead body, bring it here. Take off their clothing, put their clothing into the clothing pool, and put chlorine on them and wash them every two hours so the smell is not too bad.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I got busy with three of my friends. And on an average day-- that was the basement of the hospital on the concrete floor-- we had maybe 160 men, maybe 70, 80, 90, up to 100 women. We had to put them in alphabetic-- in numerical order. The highest number on the right, the lowest number on the left.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then by 6 o'clock, we close the shop. No more admitted that night. And we had to prepare the typed lists of men-- the following numbers from-- from 1 up to 200,000, and the A category, then the B category, all the numbers for the bookkeeping. Because they had to book all these people. They-- they are out of stock, you see. They died. So we had to put them in order.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- the reason of death? Sometimes you could see a big bruise on the head. Sometimes they were beaten heavily. Sometimes they just died from heart attack or collapsed from hunger or whatever. So that was a very good job. That was considered to be one of the best jobs in the area. I got my armband-- [GERMAN]-- official body-- I don't know what the English word is-- people who collect dead bodies.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he doubled my bread, because we had to be strong to carry bodies around and collect. Doubled the bread, gave a bit more soup. So I wasn't that hungry. Moreover, I got-- in this period I got-- sometimes I got, on Sunday mornings, an extra ticket to go to the brothel, which was the highest value. Because a ticket like that, you could sell for three onions or half a loaf of bread.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I always sold mine. I didn't-- I thought I'll leave the-- leave the brothel until-- if I survive the war. That's not so urgent. I'd rather get some bread instead. So I got my bread and I got onions and I got some cabbage. Cabbage was cheaper. Onion was-- was more expensive.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, once in a while, we got cigarettes, usually Saturday morning or Sunday morning, the good workers-- or the ones who did their job, you know, properly, like I did. I was-- being an accountant, I made beautiful lists of the dead bodies every-- every evening. And I got six or eight cigarettes a week. Well, they-- for 60 cigarettes you could buy a pair  of boots. For about 30 cigarettes you could buy a jumper, which was getting important because it was early October. It was already freezing point.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I saved up my cigarettes. I bought a jumper. And later on, when the snow was really big, I bought a pair of shoes, which saved my life later on. So I-- I changed all my brothel tickets, six tickets, and cigarettes for-- for food, jumper, and-- and boots.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then one day, the SS man said, I need somebody to translate to other languages, because a big transport of Dutch Jews came in. They could all speak English, and the French Jews, of course, French, and the Hungarians, you know. And then the-- the orders are given in German. So I need somebody who speaks Hungarian, apart from German, and English and French. Well, I said, oh, I can do this. They said, well, listen, you be careful, because if you're bluffing, I'll-- I'll test you very soon. You'll get shot if-- if you're telling me a lie. I said, no, I can do it. All right, we'll see. And he gave an order-- stand up here or stand up there, and I translated it. And my first big job-- I got a new armband. I was still a part-time [NON-ENGLISH]-- bodies. But main-- my main job was now interpreter to the SS of that building. We got our own SS man. And my first job was, which was a pretty-- a very responsible job, in the evening, when all the workers came back from work-- dead tired, hungry, dirty, you know, from carrying cement or concrete blocks or working in the mines-- I met them all. And all the sick people-- and nearly everybody was sick. Must have been 600-- I'm just guessing, 600, 600, 800 people standing at night in front of the hospital trying to get in, either for treatment or for-- to get in because they had high temperatures, you know, coughing, and they got bronchitis and pneumonia, all sorts of sicknesses, terrible sicknesses. They were all very sick. They should have been all admitted. But my order was let in six men every half an hour. And during the whole evening, I think the average admittance was about maybe 30, 35 at the most, out of 800.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my job-- and the other boy-- we were standing out there with our brand-new striped uniform with, you know, this [? thing. ?] And I asked people in English, French, where are you from? Poland? You speak English? Yeah, sure. What is your matter with you? Oh, I'm-- I'm dizzy. I got very high temp. I had orders. I can only let people in if their temperature is over 39.6. If it's only 39, that is still fit to go to work and carry bags of cement.

speaker: interviewee
text: And people who had perforated ulcers-- if their stomach was hanging out, the blood running from the stomach-- that I could let in. I could let that in. And they had people with hole in their stomach. The ulcer burned through their stomach and hanging out the intestines. I could let them in.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I let-- well, I tried to pick out the-- the most sick people, but they were all very sick. And then I wouldn't let that in or this in. And then they shouted at me, says, you bastard, can't you see how sick I am? Why don't you let me in? I'll fix you up after the war is over, you know. I'll fix you up.

speaker: interviewee
text: I said, look-- I tried explain to them. I'd like to let you all of you in, but I-- I have my-- that there's no room and only two doctors there. I can only let six people in. And now, I'm-- I'm coming back tomorrow. If you won't let me in, I'll bash your head in after the war, you know. They couldn't bash me in then because I was stronger than he was, because I-- I had double food and I wasn't working all day. He was working all day. It was a very terrible job because I understood them, that they were all very sick. But I-- I couldn't help it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that-- that was-- so here we are. To summarize the position, that-- I-- I-- I was out carrying the bags of cement in June and July. But from-- from August on, I didn't work any hard work. That's how I stayed alive up-- up till then.

speaker: interviewee
text: [? And ?] thanks to the infection of my foot and thanks to the experimentation by the doctors, I had now this wonderful, highly privileged job. And it was already middle of winter. It was heavy snow and minus 20. People outside working in their cotton uniforms. Uniforms. Terrific amount of people dying and sick. All the sick people all-- all around me all the time. One thing I could do. One of my friends who was with the potato delivery section, he-- he could-- he could-- could fill up the sleeve of his jacket with potatoes, put a string around it, then just put it on his back. He could smuggle in up to seven or eight potatoes on some evenings when he worked with potatoes. And he-- he came to the hospital, to my place. And I had in all of the rooms, I had a little-- not I, but the hospital-- had a little oven, which was mostly used to cook the dinner for the SS men. But this little oven, it-- it was, you know, with wood-- we had wood, which we could burn there. Anyhow, and so he brought the potatoes, and I made the fire. And then we had 20 minutes in between where we could make boiled potatoes, or we could just half-boil them, you know. It wasn't quite raw. So-- so he gave me four potatoes to eat, and he had the other four. And maybe he had four more sometimes, which he took back to his friend to the-- his-- and then he had to rush away. And thanks, Frank, I'll-- see if I can bring you some tomorrow. And he told me another story, which [? will end ?] of some interest, how they got potatoes in his block sometimes. Some-- there was a-- there was a deal that the people who had to go to get the so-called coffee, which was just black-- black water. It wasn't coffee, but it was-- it looked like coffee, anyhow. It was black, hot water. And they rushed to the kitchen every morning to get a barrel full with this hot water. And on some days, the deal was that the-- the man in the kitchen, he put in five kilograms of potatoes on the bottom of the coffee. He filled it up in the coffee and in-- in exchange to get-- to get some gold teeth. Somebody had to sacrifice his gold teeth, so give him two gold teeth. And for that, he got the potatoes in the coffee. And so that was a very good racket while it was going. They had a cut. The kitchen had a cut. Everybody was happy. And they got the potatoes, which they could cook it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's how we come to the end of the year. So end of 1944, I'm still the interpreter. I'm still making the list of the dead bodies. And everything was going well, except that the Russian Army was coming nearer and nearer us. We knew it because the SS man always gave us his paper in disgust, that, [GERMAN], the-- the Russians are nearer again.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we came up to the end of-- the 31st of December, the New Year's Eve. And there was to be a big party. The SS was was organizing it. And the usual-- the Germans, they celebrate New Year's Eve like-- just like here. And to my big-- big surprise, apart from the 20 SS and-- and some German Christian block commanders, about four or five Jewish people got invited. One was the doctor. Other one was a dentist. The other one was a-- a jewelry maker, who made from the stolen gold the-- the rings for the SS men's wives for birthdays and wedding anniversaries. And I was invited as the interpreter and the man in charge of the dead bodies list. I got invited. So you-- you could come, and-- and we came. And so there was New Year's Eve. It was big snow everywhere. Previously, we went-- we went to the movies. There was a movie, where they played right up to the very end how victorious the Germans Army is. We-- we shortened the lines. We-- we went back to trap the Russians. We retreated 100 miles, but only to-- in order to-- to get the Russians surrounded later. And the-- and Hitler knows what he's doing. We shorten our front, which was very beneficial for Germany. And so each time they shortened the line, this was always 200 kilometers more to the-- to the West, near-- nearer to Berlin each time.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, we went to the movie in the big snow, and I came to New Year's Eve party. And the SS man there at the table, laid out with French champagne and salami and Hungarian paprika, pate, and everything, and they said-- they said, eat, Jew, eat, eat, because it'll be a good year for you, Jew. Probably you will be liberated in a few months' time. The war is finished. Germany lost.

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he was drunk. The SS man next to me, he was drinking the champagne. He was-- they were all drunk, but he was particularly drunk. He said-- and he says, ah, it's all right for you. It's good to be a Jew now because you'll be free soon.

speaker: interviewee
text: But what will happen to us? Who knows what they will do with us, because they don't like us very much, you know. I mean, after all, we did some bad things. But it wasn't our fault. He said, I got nothing against the Jews. I quite like them, in fact. They're quite funny people. And I have nothing against them. But still, you know, order is order. I did some bad things, I suppose, but-- So actually, he was building up this friendship to me so in case the-- they come in like with the-- I could say a good, few kind words for him later on, I suppose. He was drunk. But so anyhow, I-- I just agreed to everything, and I kept eating the salami sandwiches. So I had never had so much food in the previous nine months, like I said, I had on that one evening, which was a good thing because I built up my strength a bit.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so the life went on as usual. It was January, January '45. And then we got the papers, and we could see that Krakow-- Krakow-- the big city of Krakow has fallen to the Russians, and they were advancing. And the big, huge winter offense-- [GERMAN] started. The attack started. And then we didn't know what will happen. We thought that either they will kill us all. We didn't know what will be. But we had some-- we were scared of the future anyhow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could we stop now?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we arrived to the middle of January. So we did some progress.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. That's the end of tape 3 of the interview with Frank Gipps. It's the 20th of March, 1995. We're in Sydney, Australia.
